text:
The estimated 33,000 bricks, inscribed with the names of citizens who paid $19.88 each, can't be removed without destroying them, say officials

You can save this article by registering for freehere. Orsign-inif you have an account.

While officials promise the intent is to honour the public space’s history, the upcoming rejuvenation of Olympic Plaza won’t include the tens of thousands of engraved bricks that currently encircle the downtown landmark — a legacy of the 1988 Winter Games.

Officials from the Calgary Municipal Land Corp. (CMLC), City of Calgary and Arts Commons provided an update on the $70-million Olympic Plaza transformation project Tuesday, where they confirmed that incorporating the personalized bricks into the newly designed plaza would be too expensive and arduous.

CMLC president and CEO Kate Thompson said officials looked into either reincorporating the 30,000-plus bricks into the design or removing and returning them to their purchasers. But due to their age and condition, she said individually excavating the bricks is not possible without damaging them, and would also come with a significant cost and delay to the project schedule.

“While they look fantastic, to take them out is a huge expense and not necessarily (guaranteed to be) a successful endeavour,” she said.

“I think Calgarians would want us to make sure we’re being mindful of how we use our funds and how we approach this. Taking them out individually has proven to be quite an exercise, costly and not a guarantee.”

The estimated 33,000 bricks are inscribed with the names of citizens who purchased them in 1987 for$19.88 each as a way to support Calgary hosting the 1988 Winter Olympics.

While they’ve been a mainstay in the plaza for nearly 40 years, the fate of the bricks hasbeen in question since 2016,when the city first engaged the public on plans to revitalize Olympic Plaza — a public space that Thompson has likened to being downtown’s living room.

As an alternative way to honour the bricks’ legacy, she said Calgarians are invited to six events dubbed Brick by Brick throughout September. She said CMLC officials will be on site to help Calgarians find their engraved brick, anda photographer will capture a commemorative photo.

CMLC will also provide an opportunity for Calgarians to take home a name rubbing of their brick inscription, Thompson added.

Thom Mahler, Calgary’s director of downtown strategy, said the city recognizes the bricks hold sentimental value.

To memorialize them, he noted the city has created a digitalized model onmaps.calgary.ca, and has created the Olympic Brick Finder tool that Calgarians can use to see a high-resolution photo of each brick.

“It is really important we recognize and honour the investment Calgarians made in these bricks,” he said. “As we’ve heard, it’s going to be very difficult, if not impossible, to remove these bricks without them crumbling.”

Though the bricks won’t be part of the new-look Olympic Plaza, officials say the design team intends to incorporate other elements of the space’s history, such as the skating rink that becomes a popular hub during the winter.

Thompson said CMLC’s months-long public engagement this year — more than 3,000 Calgarians responded to a survey — revealed that most residents want to see the rink maintained.

Respondents also said they want ample green space, lighting and live performance possibilities incorporated into the rejuvenated plaza.

“This space is used by so many different communities and groups to celebrate,” Thompson said. “We want to make that as easy as possible and as adaptable as possible, so people can come down here, host a festival, have a great concert and also just come down on Saturday to have a coffee and gather.”

The reimagination of Olympic Plaza is part of a larger $660-million transformation of Arts Commons, which includes a new concert venue on the west side.

In March, Premier Danielle Smith announced the provincewould contribute more than $100 milliontoward the coinciding projects, stating the rejuvenations would help breathe new life into Calgary’s downtown and arts scene.

The Olympic Plaza project has an anticipated budget of $70 million, according to CMLC, of which $40 million has already been allocated through the city’s 2023-2026 budget for downtown revitalization.

“Efforts are underway to secure the remaining required funds, bolstered by recent announcements of support for the transformation of the Arts Commons campus by the Government of Alberta and the Werklund family,” CMLC stated in a news release.

The plaza is set to close at the end of December. Construction will get underway in 2025 and is expected to last through 2028 to facilitate both its transformation and the construction of the Arts Commons expansion.

The design for the plaza is set to be revealed in early 2025, according to CMLC.

Alex Sarian, the president and CEO of Arts Commons, said the project is an opportunity to redefine what cultural and civic activities look like in the space.

“The idea for Arts Commons to be able to challenge itself by incorporating Olympic Plaza into this new campus is incredibly exciting,” he said.

“We have an opportunity as we design it differently and operate it differently to say, not only is this a living room, but it’s also a gateway to downtown.”

sstrasser@postmedia.com

title: New Olympic Plaza won't incorporate engraved bricks from 1988 Games | Calgary Herald
description: The 33,000 bricks, inscribed with the names of citizens who paid $19.88 each, can't be removed without destroying them, say officials
